title: Burp Comparer — Diff Broken Exploits vs Working Requests
body:
When a public PoC fails but the same operation works in the browser, the fastest path to understanding why is a side-by-side diff. Burp Comparer does this in two clicks: send both requests to it, compare by words or bytes, and the differences pop out highlighted.

This is the workflow that fixed Sona's broken nexus_repo_manager exploit: PoC returned 403, but logging in with the same creds in a browser returned 204. Comparer revealed the PoC had a stale hardcoded IP (192.168.1.1) and the wrong password — both invisible in raw response text, both immediately obvious in the diff.

Use this when:
  - A modified PoC returns an unexpected status code (403, 400, 500)
  - The login step fails but you know the creds work
  - The exploit's first request succeeds but the second one breaks
  - Two seemingly identical requests behave differently
  - You want to confirm session tokens are being passed correctly
cmd:
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# SETUP — ROUTE BOTH REQUESTS THROUGH BURP
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════

# In the exploit script (Python requests):
proxies = {
    'http':  'http://127.0.0.1:8080',
    'https': 'http://127.0.0.1:8080'
}
# Add proxies=proxies to EVERY s.post()/s.get() call:
r = s.post(URL + '/login', data=body, proxies=proxies, verify=False)
#                                       ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^

# For curl-based PoCs:
curl -x http://127.0.0.1:8080 -k ...

# For PowerShell (rare in OSCP web):
# $proxy = New-Object System.Net.WebProxy('http://127.0.0.1:8080')

# Confirm Burp is intercepting:
#  - Proxy → Intercept ON (or HTTP history tab shows the request)
#  - For seeing RESPONSES: Proxy → Options → Intercept Server Responses → enable

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# WORKFLOW — TWO-CLICK COMPARE
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════

# 1. Run the broken exploit. Find its request in HTTP history.
# 2. Open Burp's embedded browser. Perform the same action manually (e.g. log in).
# 3. Find the browser's working request in HTTP history.
# 4. Right-click broken request → 'Send to Comparer (request)'
# 5. Right-click working request → 'Send to Comparer (request)'
# 6. Comparer tab → both items appear → click 'Words' (or 'Bytes' for raw)
#
# Result: side-by-side diff with colored highlights.
#   - Yellow blocks = differences
#   - Click each yellow block to navigate
#   - 'Sync views' keeps both panes aligned while scrolling

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# WHAT TO LOOK FOR
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Common differences that break PoCs:
#   - Host header → exploit has hardcoded localhost/private IP
#   - Authorization → wrong creds, wrong format (Basic vs Bearer)
#   - Cookie → missing session token, stale value
#   - Content-Type → 'application/json' vs 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded'
#   - User-Agent → some apps check this (rare)
#   - Referer / Origin → CSRF defenses
#   - Body encoding → base64 vs plaintext, JSON vs form-data
#   - Trailing characters → \r\n vs \n, missing newlines

# When the REQUESTS look identical but RESPONSES differ:
#   Send both RESPONSES to Comparer too. The diff there tells you whether
#   the server is actually treating them differently, or your code is
#   misinterpreting an identical response.

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# BEYOND COMPARER — RELATED BURP DEBUGGING TOOLS
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Repeater — modify and re-send a single request:
#   Right-click → Send to Repeater
#   Edit fields directly, click Send, see the response
#   Faster than re-running the python script every time

# Decoder — base64, URL, HTML, hex:
#   Right-click highlighted bytes → Send to Decoder
#   Useful for Nexus payloads that base64-encode the credentials

# Logger — full history without intercept:
#   Sometimes easier than scrolling HTTP history. Search by URL/method.

# Extension: Logger++ — even better search/filter than built-in Logger.

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# THE SONA WORKFLOW (CONCRETE EXAMPLE)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Symptoms: exploit returns 403 on login step.
# 1. Browser login with same creds: 204 No Content (success)
# 2. Both → Comparer (request) → Words
# 3. Differences highlighted:
#      Host: 192.168.1.1:8081     vs    Host: 192.168.45.159:8081
#      password=cGFzc3dvcmQ=      vs    password=M2U0MDllODktN...
# 4. Fix both in exploit.py → re-run → 204 success → exploit chain continues
#
# Total debug time: ~90 seconds. Without Comparer: 20 minutes of squinting at logs.
warn: Comparer doesn't fix exploits — it tells you what's different. If the broken request has the right Host, Cookie, and body but still fails, the difference is in TIMING (a token expired between login and exploit) or STATE (the working session had been warmed up by an earlier request). When the diff is empty but behaviors differ, look at the full session sequence in HTTP history.

choices.label: Found the difference — fix and re-run exploit
Diff was empty but behavior differs — state/timing issue
